name: Equilibrium Analysis
summary: A systematic approach to finding the state where all forces, torques, or flows balance — the system is at rest or in steady state.
whenToUseIt: Use equilibrium analysis for statics problems, thermodynamic equilibrium, chemical equilibrium, and any system that has settled into a stable or steady state.
steps: Identify the system and draw a free-body diagram (or equivalent for thermal/chemical systems)
List all forces, torques, or flows acting on the system
Set the net force (or net torque, or net flow) equal to zero
Solve the resulting equations for unknown quantities
Check stability: if the system is displaced slightly, does it return to equilibrium (stable) or move further away (unstable)?
example: A ladder leans against a frictionless wall. Forces: gravity (centre of mass), normal force from wall (top), normal force from floor (bottom), friction from floor (bottom). Setting sum of forces = 0 and sum of torques = 0 gives the minimum angle before the ladder slips.
